item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My only real issue was that the dialogue during the expedition was a little janky here and there. The D-Class felt a little unnatural and Control could have been more clinical here and there, as well as the description of the actions taken during the expedition.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the effects are library-themed, given how they react to disruptive behaviour in the library.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find the effects a little random and the tone a little Series I-y, but overall it's interesting enough to upvote. I think the exploration log is what seals the deal, although the D-class being uncooperative and profane is kind of getting old in general.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the compliment :D

People ask that too. I think maybe at night, there aren't many people to disturb so you can talk louder :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awesome read, although I don't really understand, that the effect where if you speak too loud you can't talk for 30 seconds, but during the exploration, it seems that the D-Class member talk perfectly fine, why is that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The pieces are now more cohesive, less disjointed, and reads more like a Gothic short fiction.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
;~;

Any tips for cracking your emotions next time?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a perfectly well written, well executed example of an SCP article, and it just does nothing whatsoever for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, thanks for the compliment :D

But actually, the pocket dimension is the librarian's actual room, where she do the reincarnation stuff. The rest of the library have normal topology.

Unless you are affected by that building that used to be a library again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an satisfying account of a perfectly normal building that used to be a library.

Flawed, as a former library worker myself, this was quite enjoyable; the spoilers helped. The sudden transition of the D-class to a pocket dimension characteristic of a building that used to be a library was unexpected and interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After two years of reading and a month of drafting and fixing, finally, my first skip is out.
Great thanks to:

  • Zyn for polishing the idea, character development and critting!
  • Tufto and The Great Hippo for advices and two major rewrites of the storyline!
  • Garckbloom, GibberingEloquence, SunnyClockwork, Rabid, Larpnochez, noxfero, MrWrong and so so much more in chat for advices on the story, character development, tone, etc
  • Phyre, Tufto in chat and my friend for grammar checking!
  • Fingo7 for advices on the forum!
  • Catblob for compliment!
  • TUFTO again for the badass name

(Seriously, I’ve asked like 30 people just for this one)
Images:

  • The first is from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Carnegie_Library_Jacksonville.jpg
  • The second is from https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Noblewoman_from_Grodno_province_-_Russian_empire_-_1864_AD.jpg
  • The third one is made by myself using my mediocre Blender skills :P

Explanation:

SPOILERS
Hide

Essentially, this librarian reincarnates dead people as books. But the one in her room is a WIP special version, designed to bring her husband and son back. Imagine SCP-963 as a book.

The reason for the other books to induce the memetic effect is like a way for them to be read, to be remembered. And it must be the civilians of the town who read them, because those are the close ones, even close relatives of the dead.

The reason the civilians accept the anomalous nature of the library is because they are unknowingly reconciling with their lost ones.
